key_insight: Kr+ ion implantation can reproducibly create stable, room-temperature near-infrared spin defects in hBN without any annealing.
engineering_relevance: This provides a scalable, chemically inert defect-engineering route for quantum-photonic devices based on hBN.
method: Optimized 40 keV Kr+ implantation conditions using SRIM Monte Carlo simulations.
Implanted hBN flakes across fluences from 10^11 to 10^15 ions/cm^2 and measured PL, Raman, and temperature dependence.
Used EPR and DFT to link the optical/magnetic response to a V_N–C_B donor-acceptor pair defect complex.
evidence_signal: The implanted samples showed a stable ~830 nm PL band that increased with fluence, plus a g = 2.003 EPR signal and a defect Raman feature near 1295 cm^-1.
watch_item: Defect identity and optical performance may depend strongly on implantation dose and lattice damage, so process control will be critical for device reproducibility.
paper_title: Hexagonal Boron Nitride Spin Defects for Quantum Photonics: Annealing-Free Generation by Krypton Ion Implantation
